task: I’m about to hand off a finished edit and want to make sure I’m not missing anything obvious before I send it. Can you help me build a quick last-pass checklist for picture, sound, and exports that’s realistic for a small team and not too bloated?

prefs.explanation: He seems to value getting to the point without extra noise, especially given how he works under deadline pressure. A response that is too long or meandering would likely feel inefficient to him. He probably still wants enough detail to act, but not a lot of filler.

His communication style in work is straightforward: explain the issue, name the delay, and state the next step. He likely prefers the same from an assistant rather than softened hints or vague hedging. Directness fits his honest, exacting temperament.

He seems to respond well to order and clean workflow, so scattered or exploratory replies would probably frustrate him. Structured guidance would match how he already thinks about edits, revisions, and process templates. He likely appreciates when the assistant lays things out in a predictable sequence.

prefs.short: Wants the main points plus key caveats
prefs.explanation: He cares about technical correctness, so bare headlines are probably not enough when a mistake could matter. At the same time, he likely does not want exhaustive theory unless it directly helps him act. A focused answer with the important caveats is the best fit.

prefs.short: Responds best to direct, constructive critique
prefs.explanation: He appears to prefer honest correction over vague reassurance, especially when quality or timing is at stake. Pointing out what is wrong and how to fix it would likely be more useful to him than overly gentle framing. He probably respects critique that is specific and practical.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a lean **last-pass handoff checklist** for a small team.

## Final handoff checklist

### Picture
- Confirm the latest approved version is the one being sent
- Check for any temp shots, temp text, placeholders, or guide slugs still in the cut
- Verify titles, lower thirds, end cards, and legal text are spelled correctly
- Watch for black frames, flash frames, missing shots, or accidental gaps
- Make sure any notes from the last review are actually resolved

### Sound
- Check for obvious sync issues
- Listen for pops, clicks, clipped peaks, or abrupt fades
- Confirm temp music or temp VO isn’t still being treated like final
- Make sure dialogue levels are consistent enough for review
- Check that no clips are muted, offline, or accidentally doubled

### Export
- Confirm export settings match delivery spec
- Check file name, version number, and date
- Verify runtime matches the timeline
- Spot-check the export for picture and audio
- Make sure the correct aspect ratio, frame rate, and resolution were used

### Before sending
- Save a clean backup of the project
- Keep the handoff folder clearly labeled
- Include a short note with:
  - version
  - what’s included
  - what’s pending, if anything
  - any known issues

If you want, I can also turn this into:
- a **checkbox version**
- a **client-facing handoff note**
- or a **more specific checklist for Premiere / Avid / Resolve**

per_pref.reason: Fairly concise and efficient: it says "here’s a lean last-pass handoff checklist" and stays focused, with no obvious filler. It does include a few extra options at the end, but overall it’s tight.
Very direct and plain: "Confirm," "Check," "Verify," and "Make sure" are used throughout, with clear, actionable statements and no hedging.
Strongly structured: it’s organized into clear sections — "Picture," "Sound," "Export," and "Before sending" — with bullet points under each, which matches a step-by-step workflow.
Gives the main points plus useful caveats, like "temp shots," "black frames," "clipped peaks," and matching delivery specs. It’s practical without becoming overly exhaustive.

The checklist is direct and practical, and it flags concrete problems to fix. It doesn’t explicitly critique the user’s work, but it does point out what to look for and what could be wrong in a constructive way.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a tighter **5-minute last-pass checklist** split into **internal handoff** vs **client send**.

## Internal handoff
- Confirm the correct timeline/version is the one being exported
- Make sure all last-minute fixes are actually in the timeline
- Check for temp shots, temp text, placeholders, or guide slugs still present
- Verify legal text, titles, lower thirds, and end cards are correct
- Make sure no obvious gaps, flash frames, or missing shots remain
- Confirm handles are correct if this is going to online or color
- Save a clean project backup before export

## Client send
- Export the correct format, frame rate, resolution, and aspect ratio
- Check file name and version number
- Verify runtime matches the intended cut
- Spot-check the export for picture and sound
- Confirm audio channel mapping / stereo downmix is correct
- Listen for sync issues, pops, clicks, clipped peaks, or abrupt fades
- Make sure temp music or temp VO is not reading as final
- Check title safe / action safe if the delivery needs it
- Add burned-in timecode or slates if requested or required

## Quick QC pass
- Watch the export once, start to finish, with sound on
- Check that the fixes you made are visible in the export
- Confirm nothing broke in render, downmix, or export settings

## Before sending
- Label the handoff folder clearly
- Include a short note with:
  - version
  - format
  - what’s included
  - what’s pending, if anything
  - any known issues

If you want, I can turn this into a **super-short 10-bullet version** you can keep by your export window.

per_pref.reason: Mostly concise and efficient: it offers a "tighter 5-minute last-pass checklist" and a short closing offer for an even shorter version. Slightly longer than necessary, but not bloated.
Very direct and plain: "Confirm the correct timeline/version," "Check file name and version number," "Listen for sync issues". It states actions clearly without hedging.
Well organized and step-by-step, with clear sections: "Internal handoff," "Client send," "Quick QC pass," and "Before sending." The sequence is predictable and easy to follow.

Gives the main points plus key caveats without overexplaining: includes important checks like "audio channel mapping / stereo downmix," "sync issues," and "title safe / action safe" while staying focused.

Constructive and practical rather than soft: it points out what to verify and what can go wrong, e.g. "temp shots," "missing shots," "pops, clicks, clipped peaks," and "what's pending." It is helpful, though not especially critical in tone.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s the **dumb-fast 10-line version**.

## Last-pass checklist

### Timeline / picture
- [ ] Right version on the timeline
- [ ] Last fixes are baked in
- [ ] No temp text, slugs, or placeholders
- [ ] No flash frames, gaps, or missing shots
- [ ] Titles / lower thirds / legal read clean
- [ ] Safe action/title checked if required

### Audio / QC
- [ ] Channel mapping is correct
- [ ] Stereo fold-down sounds right
- [ ] No clipped peaks, pops, clicks, or abrupt fades
- [ ] Sync holds through the export
- [ ] No temp music or temp VO left in as final

### Export / delivery
- [ ] Correct codec, frame rate, resolution, and aspect
- [ ] Runtime matches expectation
- [ ] Handles included if going to online/color
- [ ] Burn-in TC / slate added if requested
- [ ] File name, version, and date are correct
- [ ] Export is spot-checked, not just the timeline

### Handoff
- [ ] Internal: project backup + clean folder + notes saved
- [ ] Client: approved export only + short handoff note

If you want, I can make this even tighter into a **one-screen version** with just the checkboxes and no headings.

per_pref.reason: Very concise and efficient: opens with "the dumb-fast 10-line version" and uses a compact checklist with minimal filler.
Direct and plain: items are stated plainly like "No temp text" and "Correct codec, frame rate, resolution, and aspect," with no hedging.
Well organized and step-by-step: clear sections for "Timeline / picture," "Audio / QC," "Export / delivery," and "Handoff" make it easy to follow.
Gives the main points plus key caveats: covers essential checks and important specifics like "Safe action/title checked if required" and "Export is spot-checked, not just the timeline."
Constructive and practical: it identifies what to check and how to fix/avoid issues, though it is more checklist-oriented than explicitly critical.
